activities_projects.title: Investigating Inter-particle Space
activities_projects.objective: To demonstrate that particles of matter have space between them[cite: 481, 484].
steps: Fill a beaker with 50 mL of water and mark the level[cite: 431].
Add a measured quantity of salt and dissolve it by stirring with a glass rod[cite: 432].
Observe the final water level and note any changes[cite: 433].
Repeat with a larger quantity of salt until no more dissolves.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that salt particles get into the spaces between water particles[cite: 442, 506].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why the volume does not increase significantly initially (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Estimating Particle Size through Dilution
activities_projects.objective: To visualize how small the particles of matter are[cite: 443].
steps: Dissolve 2 crystals of $KMnO_{4}$ in 100 mL of water[cite: 446].
Transfer 10 mL of this solution into 90 mL of clear water[cite: 450].
Repeat this dilution process up to 8 times[cite: 452].
Compare the color of the final beaker with the first one[cite: 474].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Proves that a few crystals contain millions of tiny particles[cite: 476, 479].
activities_projects.evaluation: Discuss the limit of visibility in the dilution process (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Thermal Energy and Kinetic Theory
activities_projects.objective: To observe the relationship between temperature and particle movement[cite: 504].
steps: Drop a crystal into a glass of hot water and another into cold water[cite: 493].
Do not stir; allow crystals to settle[cite: 494].
Observe the spread of color just above the crystal over time[cite: 495, 496].
Measure the time taken for the color to reach the top in both cases.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Confirms that kinetic energy of particles increases with temperature[cite: 504, 806].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain the change in the rate of mixing with temperature (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Diffusion Rates in Different Fluids
activities_projects.objective: To compare the rate of diffusion in liquids of different densities[cite: 507, 616].
steps: Drop ink slowly along the side of the first beaker[cite: 486].
Drop honey in the same way into the second beaker[cite: 486].
Record observations immediately and after several hours[cite: 488, 490].
Compare the uniformity of color/texture in both beakers.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies that intermixing occurs on its own by particles getting into spaces[cite: 505, 506].
activities_projects.evaluation: Analyze why honey takes longer to diffuse than ink (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Force of Attraction Model
activities_projects.objective: To understand that strength of attraction varies between substances[cite: 533].
steps: Try breaking each item by hammering, cutting, or stretching[cite: 517].
Note which substance requires the most force to break[cite: 518].
Compare these results to a 'human chain' model of particles[cite: 512, 513].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that forces between particles keep matter together[cite: 532, 843].
activities_projects.evaluation: Rank the materials by the strength of their inter-particle forces (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: State Properties and Rigidity
activities_projects.objective: To classify articles based on shape, boundaries, and volume[cite: 557].
steps: Sketch the shape of solid articles and check for definite boundaries[cite: 556, 561].
Attempt to compress the solids by applying force[cite: 560].
Pour 50 mL of water into different shaped containers and check volume[cite: 581, 582].
Verify if the liquid maintains its shape or flows[cite: 583, 584].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Distinguishes between rigid solids and fluid liquids[cite: 563, 587].
activities_projects.evaluation: Tabulate differences between solid and liquid states (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Compressibility and Particle Distance
activities_projects.objective: To compare inter-particle distances in states of matter[cite: 745, 757].
steps: Seal syringe nozzles and fill them with air, water, and chalk respectively[cite: 603, 605].
Apply vaseline to pistons for smooth movement[cite: 606].
Push the pistons and measure the distance moved[cite: 607].
Calculate the 'compressibility factor' based on piston movement[cite: 608].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Observes that gases have the most space between particles[cite: 596, 616].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why gases are used in LPG and CNG cylinders (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Latent Heat and Change of State
activities_projects.objective: To study temperature behavior during melting and boiling[cite: 682, 686].
steps: Heat ice and record the temperature at which it starts melting[cite: 682].
Note the temperature until all ice has converted to water[cite: 683, 697].
Continue heating until the water boils and vaporizes[cite: 685, 686].
Plot a time-temperature graph for the entire process.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understand that heat is used to overcome forces of attraction (Latent Heat)[cite: 700, 701].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why temperature remains constant during change of state (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Sublimation vs. Deposition
activities_projects.objective: To observe state changes that bypass the liquid phase[cite: 724].
steps: Place crushed camphor in a china dish with an inverted funnel[cite: 727, 728].
Seal the funnel stem with cotton and heat slowly[cite: 729, 742].
Observe camphor vapors and the solidified camphor on the funnel walls[cite: 730, 733].
Identify which part of the process is sublimation and which is deposition[cite: 743].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Defines the direct interconversion between solid and gaseous states[cite: 743, 849].
activities_projects.evaluation: Compare these results with the melting of ice (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Factors Affecting Evaporation Rate
activities_projects.objective: To analyze how external conditions influence evaporation[cite: 790].
steps: Put 5 mL water in a test tube and 5 mL in a china dish near a fan[cite: 794, 795].
Put 5 mL in another china dish inside a closed cupboard[cite: 796].
Record the time/days taken for evaporation in each case[cite: 798].
Repeat on a rainy day to check the effect of humidity[cite: 799].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Determines that surface area, wind speed, and humidity affect evaporation[cite: 801, 803, 807, 810].
activities_projects.evaluation: Predict the evaporation rate for a larger surface area like a floor (10 marks).
